name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنَآ إِلَىٰٓ ءَادَمَ مِن قَبْلُ فَنَسِىَ وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُۥ عَزْمًا (١١٥)
```

```english
Certainly We had enjoined Adam earlier; but he forgot, and We did not find any resoluteness in him. (115)
```

```arabic
وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِـَٔادَمَ فَسَجَدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ أَبَىٰ (١١٦)
```

```english
When We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they prostrated, but not Iblis: he refused. (116)
```

```arabic
فَقُلْنَا يَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا عَدُوٌّ لَّكَ وَلِزَوْجِكَ فَلَا يُخْرِجَنَّكُمَا مِنَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ فَتَشْقَىٰٓ (١١٧)
```

```english
We said, ‘O Adam! This is indeed an enemy of yours and your mate’s. So do not let him expel you from paradise, or you will be miserable. (117)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ لَكَ أَلَّا تَجُوعَ فِيهَا وَلَا تَعْرَىٰ (١١٨)
```

```english
You will neither be hungry in it nor naked. (118)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَّكَ لَا تَظْمَؤُا۟ فِيهَا وَلَا تَضْحَىٰ (١١٩)
```

```english
You will neither be thirsty in it, nor suffer from [the heat of] the sun.’ (119)
```

```arabic
فَوَسْوَسَ إِلَيْهِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ قَالَ يَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ هَلْ أَدُلُّكَ عَلَىٰ شَجَرَةِ ٱلْخُلْدِ وَمُلْكٍ لَّا يَبْلَىٰ (١٢٠)
```

```english
Then Satan tempted him. He said, ‘O Adam! Shall I show you the tree of immortality, and an imperishable kingdom?’ (120)
```

```arabic
فَأَكَلَا مِنْهَا فَبَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْءَٰتُهُمَا وَطَفِقَا يَخْصِفَانِ عَلَيْهِمَا مِن وَرَقِ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۚ وَعَصَىٰٓ ءَادَمُ رَبَّهُۥ فَغَوَىٰ (١٢١)
```

```english
So they both ate of it, and their nakedness became evident to them, and they began to stitch over themselves with the leaves of paradise. Adam disobeyed his Lord, and went amiss. (121)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ ٱجْتَبَـٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ (١٢٢)
```

```english
Then his Lord chose him, and turned to him clemently, and guided him. (122)
```

```arabic
قَالَ ٱهْبِطَا مِنْهَا جَمِيعًۢا ۖ بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ ۖ فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُم مِّنِّى هُدًى فَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ هُدَاىَ فَلَا يَضِلُّ وَلَا يَشْقَىٰ (١٢٣)
```

```english
He said, ‘Get down both of you from it, all together, being enemies of one another! Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance will not go astray, nor will they be miserable. (123)
```

```arabic
وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَن ذِكْرِى فَإِنَّ لَهُۥ مَعِيشَةً ضَنكًا وَنَحْشُرُهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ أَعْمَىٰ (١٢٤)
```

```english
But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life, and We shall raise him blind on the Day of Resurrection.’ (124)
```

```arabic
قَالَ رَبِّ لِمَ حَشَرْتَنِىٓ أَعْمَىٰ وَقَدْ كُنتُ بَصِيرًا (١٢٥)
```

```english
He will say, ‘My Lord! Why have You raised me blind, though I used to see?’ (125)
```

```arabic
قَالَ كَذَٰلِكَ أَتَتْكَ ءَايَـٰتُنَا فَنَسِيتَهَا ۖ وَكَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْيَوْمَ تُنسَىٰ (١٢٦)
```

```english
He will say: ‘So it is. Our signs came to you, but you forgot them, and so you will be forgotten today.’ (126)
```

## (Commentary)

The story of the entry of Adam and his spouse into the Garden, their departure from it due to the whispering (الوسوسة) of Satan, and Allah’s decree at that moment regarding the legislation of religion (تشريع الدين), the felicity (سعادة) of those who follow the guidance (الهدى), and the wretchedness (شقاء) of those who turn away from the remembrance of Allah (ذكر الله).

This story has been mentioned in this Surah with the most concise phrasing and the most beautiful exposition. The primary focus therein—as evidenced by the detail of its conclusion—pertains to the explanation of what He decreed regarding the legislation of religion and the recompense through reward and punishment. This is further supported by the subsequent derivation in His saying: **وَكَذلِكَ نَجْزِي مَنْ أَسْرَفَ وَلَمْ يُؤْمِنْ بِآياتِ رَبِّهِ** "Thus do We restitute him who is transgressing and does not believe in the signs of his Lord" (20:127). Indeed, the story also has a certain connection to the mention of Adam’s repentance in His previous saying: **وَإِنِّي لَغَفَّارٌ لِمَنْ تابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً ثُمَّ اهْتَدى** "Indeed I am All-forgiving toward him who repents, believes, and acts righteously, and then is guided" (20:82).

The story—as appears from its context in this Surah and others where it is mentioned, such as *al-Baqarah* and *al-A'raf*—represents the state of the human being according to his earthly, material nature (طبعه الأرضي المادي). Allah, the Glorified, created him in the best of forms, immersed him in His countless blessings, and settled him in the garden of moderation (جنة الاعتدال). He forbade him from transgression by exiting toward the side of excess (الإسراف) through following whims (اتباع الهوى), clinging to the mirage of the world, and forgetting the side of the Lord, the Exalted, by abandoning His covenant (العهد) to him, disobeying Him, and following the whispering of Satan. Satan beautifies the world for him, depicting and imagining to him that if he clings to it and forgets his Lord, he would thereby acquire mastery over cosmic causes (الأسباب الكونية), using them to subjugate all the pleasures of life he desires, and that these pleasures will remain for him and he for them.

This continues until, when he clings to the world and forgets the station of his Lord, the shameful parts (سوآت) of life appear to him, and the evils of wretchedness loom before him through the descent of calamities, the betrayal of time, the failure of causes, and the turning away of Satan from him. He then begins to stitch together the outward appearances of blessings, trying to compensate for a lost blessing with an existing one, shifting from one torment to another more severe, and treating a painful disease with another more agonizing, until he is commanded to exit the garden of blessing and dignity toward the landing-place of wretchedness and disappointment.

This is what was represented for Adam (peace be upon him) when Allah entered him into the Garden and honored him with dignity, until his affair reached what it reached. However, his incident (peace be upon him) occurred before the legislation of the foundation of religion, and his garden was an intermediate garden (جنة برزخية) — *a realm between the material and the purely spiritual* — represented in a non-worldly life. Therefore, the prohibition was advisory (إرشادي) rather than legislative (مولوي), and his contravention led to a compulsory consequence rather than a legislative punishment, as previously detailed in the commentary on Surahs *al-Baqarah* and *al-A'raf*.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنا إِلى آدَمَ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَنَسِيَ وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُ عَزْماً** **"Certainly We had enjoined Adam before; but he forgot, and We did not find any resoluteness in him" (20:115).** The meaning of the covenant (العهد) here is the injunction or testament (الوصية); it is in this sense that decrees and instructions are called "covenants." Forgetfulness (النسيان) is well-known, and it is sometimes used as a metonym for abandonment (الترك) because abandonment is its necessary consequence; for when a thing is forgotten, it is abandoned. Resoluteness (العزم) is the firm intention (القصد الجازم) toward a thing. The Exalted said: **فَإِذا عَزمْتَ فَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ** "And when you have decided, then trust in Allah" (3:159). It is also sometimes applied to patience (الصبر), perhaps because patience is difficult for the souls and requires a more deep-rooted and firm intention; thus, patience is named after its necessary concomitant. The Exalted said: **إِنَّ ذلِكَ لَمِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ** "That is indeed the course of [worthy] matters" (31:17).

The meaning, then, is: "And I swear, We enjoined Adam before, but he abandoned the injunction, and We did not find in him a firm intention to preserve it or patience upon it." The mentioned covenant—as appears from his story (peace be upon him) in various places of the speech of the Exalted—is the prohibition against eating from the tree, such as His saying: **لا تَقْرَبا هذِهِ الشَّجَرَةَ** "Do not approach this tree" (7:19).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذْ قُلْنا لِلْمَلائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ أَبى** **"And when We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they prostrated, but not Iblis: he refused" (20:116).** This is coordinated to an omitted phrase, the estimation being: "Mention Our covenant to him, and mention the time We commanded the angels to prostrate to him, and they prostrated except Iblis," so that it becomes clear that he forgot and did not resolve to keep the injunction. His saying: **أَبى** "he refused" is an answer to an implied question: "What did Iblis do?" It was said: "He refused."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَقُلْنا يا آدَمُ إِنَّ هذا عَدُوٌّ لَكَ وَلِزَوْجِكَ فَلا يُخْرِجَنَّكُما مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ فَتَشْقى** **"So We said, ‘O Adam! This is indeed an enemy to you and your mate. So do not let him expel you from paradise, or you will be miserable’" (20:117).** This is a derivation from Iblis’s refusal to prostrate. That is: when he refused, We said—advising Adam regarding what was for his own benefit and as a counsel—"Indeed this one who refused the prostration, Iblis, is an enemy to you and your spouse," etc.

His saying: **فَلا يُخْرِجَنَّكُما مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ** "So do not let him expel you from paradise" directs the prohibition of Iblis expelling them from the Garden toward Adam as a metonym for forbidding him from obeying Iblis or being heedless of his plot and belittling his deception. That is: "Do not obey him, and do not be heedless of his plot and enticement, lest he gain mastery over you both and become able to expel you from the Garden and make you wretched."

Imam al-Razi mentioned in his commentary several reasons for Iblis’s enmity toward Adam and his spouse, but these are trivial reasons with no benefit in detailing. The truth is that the cause was his expulsion from the Presence of Proximity (حضرة القرب), his being stoned, and the placement of the curse upon him until the Day of Resurrection, as appears from his saying—may he be cursed—as recounted by Allah: **قالَ رَبِّ بِما أَغْوَيْتَنِي لَأُزَيِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** "He said, ‘My Lord! As You have consigned me to perversity, I will surely glamorize [evil] for them on the earth, and I will surely pervert them, all together’" (15:39). And his saying: **قالَ أَرَأَيْتَكَ هذَا الَّذِي كَرَّمْتَ عَلَيَّ لَئِنْ أَخَّرْتَنِ إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ لَأَحْتَنِكَنَّ ذُرِّيَّتَهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً** "He said, ‘Tell me, this one whom You have honored above me, if You respite me until the Day of Resurrection, I will surely destroy his progeny, [all] but a few’" (18:62). It is well-known that the honoring of Adam over him is the honoring of the human species over him, just as the command to prostrate to him was a command to prostrate to the human species. Thus, the root cause is the precedence of the human and the falling behind of the devil, followed by expulsion and cursing.

His saying: **فَتَشْقى** "or you will be miserable" is a derivation from their exit from the Garden. The meaning of wretchedness (الشقاء) here is toil (التعب). That is: "You will toil if you both exit the Garden and live elsewhere, which is the earth, an earthly life where needs will attack you and you will strive to fulfill them, such as the need for food, drink, clothing, shelter, and others."

The evidence that wretchedness means toil is found in the two following verses which point to its explanation: **إِنَّ لَكَ أَلَّا تَجُوعَ فِيها وَلا تَعْرى وَأَنَّكَ لا تَظْمَؤُا فِيها وَلا تَدْحى** "Indeed you will neither be hungry in it nor naked; and you will neither be thirsty in it nor suffer from the sun" (20:118-119).

This is also evidence that the prohibition was advisory (إرشادي); its contravention results only in falling into the corruption (المفسدة) that follows the act itself, which is the toil of striving to fulfill the needs of life and acquiring a livelihood. It was not a legislative (مولوي) command where the contravention itself is a corruption the servant falls into, entailing an otherworldly reckoning.

Furthermore, you have already learned that it was a covenant before the legislation of the foundation of religion, which occurred upon the command to exit the Garden and descend to the earth.

As for the use of the singular in His saying: **فَتَشْقى** "or you will be miserable" rather than the dual form "or you both will be miserable," it is because the covenant was revealed to Adam (peace be upon him) and the address was directed to him. For this reason, the singular form was used in everything referring back to him, such as His saying: **فَنَسِيَ وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُ عَزْماً** "but he forgot, and We did not find any resoluteness in him," **فَتَشْقى** "or you will be miserable," **أَلَّا تَجُوعَ فِيها وَلا تَعْرى** "you will neither be hungry in it nor naked," **لا تَظْمَؤُا فِيها وَلا تَضْحى** "you will neither be thirsty in it nor suffer from the sun," **فَوَسْوَسَ إِلَيْهِ** "then [Satan] tempted him," etc., **وَعَصى** "and [Adam] disobeyed," etc., **ثُمَّ اجْتَباهُ رَبُّهُ فَتابَ عَلَيْهِ** "Then his Lord chose him, and He turned to him clemently." Indeed, the dual form was used where it was indispensable, such as His saying: **عَدُوٌّ لَكَ وَلِزَوْجِكَ فَلا يُخْرِجَنَّكُما** "an enemy to you and your mate. So do not let him expel you," **فَأَكَلا مِنْها فَبَدَتْ لَهُما** "So they both ate from it, and their nakedness became exposed to them," **وَطَفِقا يَخْصِفانِ عَلَيْهِما** "and they began to stitch together for themselves," **قالَ اهْبِطا مِنْها جَمِيعاً بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ** "He said, ‘Get down both of you from it, all together, being enemies of one another.’" Reflect upon this.

It has been said that the singular **فَتَشْقى** is because the maintenance of the woman is upon the man; thus, the wretchedness—which is the toil in acquiring a livelihood—was attributed to Adam. However, the two following verses do not harmonize with this; for if it were as said, it would have been: "Indeed for you both is that you shall not hunger," etc. It has also been said that the singular is for the sake of maintaining the rhyme (رعاية الفواصل), but this is as you see.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ لَكَ أَلَّا تَجُوعَ فِيها وَلا تَعْرى وَأَنَّكَ لا تَظْمَؤُا فِيها وَلا تَضْحى** **"Indeed you will neither be hungry in it nor naked; and you will neither be thirsty in it nor suffer from the sun" (20:118-119).** It is said: **ضحى يضحى** (he suffered from the sun) when the sun strikes him or he is exposed to it. It seems the meaning of not suffering from the sun is that there is no trace of the sun's heat there such that one would need to seek shelter in a dwelling to protect against heat and cold.

These four matters have been arranged in a manner of ordered involution and evolution (اللف والنشر المرتب) to maintain the rhymes. The original order would be: "that you shall not hunger therein, nor thirst, nor be naked, nor suffer from the sun."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَوَسْوَسَ إِلَيْهِ الشَّيْطانُ قالَ يا آدَمُ هَلْ أَدُلُّكَ عَلى شَجَرَةِ الْخُلْدِ وَمُلْكٍ لا يَبْلى** **"Then Satan tempted him. He said, ‘O Adam! Shall I guide you to the tree of eternity, and a kingdom that never regresses?’" (20:120).** The Devil (الشيطان) is the evil one; Iblis was given this title due to his wickedness. The "tree of eternity" (شجرة الخلد) refers to the forbidden tree. Decay (البلى) is for a thing to become worn out, the opposite of new.

The "tree of eternity" means a tree whose eating grants eternal life. A "kingdom that never regresses" (ملك لا يبلى) means a dominion that is not affected by the passage of ages or the friction of competitors and obstacles. Thus, the meaning results in something like: "Shall I guide you to a tree by whose fruit you will be granted eternal life and a permanent kingdom?" Therefore, His saying: **لا يَبْلى** "that never regresses" is not a repetition for emphasis, as has been suggested.

The evidence for what has been mentioned is found in Surah *al-A'raf* regarding this meaning: **ما نَهاكُما رَبُّكُما عَنْ هذِهِ الشَّجَرَةِ إِلَّا أَنْ تَكُونا مَلَكَيْنِ أَوْ تَكُونا مِنَ الْخالِدِينَ** "Your Lord has only forbidden you from this tree lest you should become angels, or lest you should become immortal" (7:20). There is no contradiction between combining the eternity of life and the permanence of the kingdom here with the conjunction "and" (الواو), and the hesitation between them in Surah *al-A'raf*; for it is possible that the hesitation there was to prevent exclusion (منع الخلو) rather than to prevent combination (منع الجمع). Or, the combination here is in consideration of being characterized by both, while the hesitation there is in consideration of the attachment of the prohibition. It is as if it were said: "In this tree are two attributes, and your Lord only forbade you from it either for this one, or for that one," or "Your Lord only forbade you from it so that you do not remain eternally in the Garden with a permanent kingdom," or "so that you do not remain eternally," based on the fact that a permanent kingdom necessitates an eternal life. Understand this. In any case, there is no contradiction between the hesitation in one verse and the combination in another.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَكَلا مِنْها فَبَدَتْ لَهُما سَوْآتُهُما وَطَفِقا يَخْصِفانِ عَلَيْهِما مِنْ وَرَقِ الْجَنَّةِ** **"So they both ate from it, and their nakedness became exposed to them, and they began to stitch together for themselves some of the leaves of paradise" (20:121).** The commentary on this has already been provided in Surah *al-A'raf*.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَعَصى آدَمُ رَبَّهُ فَغَوى** **"and Adam disobeyed his Lord, and went amiss" (20:121).** Error (الغي) is the opposite of right-guidance (الرشد), which means hitting the mark of reality. It is different from straying (الضلال), which is to exit the path. Guidance (الهدى) stands opposite to both. It means "directing" (الإرشاد) when it opposes error, as in the following verse, and it means "showing the way" or "conveying to the goal" by setting the path when it opposes straying. Thus, it is not acceptable to interpret error (الغي) in the verse as straying (الضلال).

The disobedience (معصية) of Adam toward his Lord—as we just pointed out and as previously detailed—was only the disobedience of an advisory (إرشادي) command, not a legislative (مولوي) one. The Prophets (peace be upon them) are infallible (معصومون) from disobedience and contravention in matters pertaining to the religion revealed to them: in terms of receiving it, they do not err; in terms of preserving it, they do not forget or distort; and in terms of conveying it to the people, they do not speak except the truth revealed to them, and their actions do not contradict their words. They do not commit any sin, small or large, because in action there is a conveyance just as in speech. As for disobedience in the sense of contravening an advisory command—where there is no motive except for the commanded to secure a good or benefit from the goods and benefits of his life by choosing the most proper path, just as a sincere advisor commands and forbids—then obedience and disobedience to it fall outside the scope of the proofs of infallibility (العصمة). This is evident.

Let this be the meaning of the saying that the Prophets (peace be upon them), despite their infallibility, may "abandon the better" (ترك الأولى), and among this was Adam’s (peace be upon him) eating from the tree. This verse is a battlefield of opinions, and commentaries have differed regarding it according to their schools of thought concerning the infallibility of the Prophets, each pulling the fire toward his own loaf.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ اجْتَباهُ رَبُّهُ فَتابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدى** **"Then his Lord chose him, and He turned to him clemently, and guided him" (20:122).** Choosing (الاجتباء)—as mentioned repeatedly—means gathering in the way of selection (الاصطفاء). It involves the Exalted gathering His servant for Himself, such that no one shares him with Him, making him among the purified (المخلصين). Upon this meaning is derived His saying: **فَتابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدى** "and He turned to him clemently, and guided him." It is as if he had scattered, dispersed parts, and He gathered them from here and there to one place, then turned toward him, returned to him, guided him, and led him to Himself.

We have interpreted His saying: **هَدى** "guided him"—which is absolute—as His guiding him to Himself by the context of "choosing" (الاجتباء). This does not contradict the absolute nature of guidance, because guidance to the Exalted is the root and origin of all guidance. Indeed, guidance must be restricted to what pertains to the matter of religion, such as true belief and righteous deeds. The proof for this is the derivation of guidance in the verse from "choosing." Understand this.

Accordingly, the objection does not apply to what we previously stated—that the appearance of this guidance occurring after the mention of that "going amiss" (الغواية) implies that the type of that error was removed from him. Since it was an error in an advisory matter, the verse indicates the granting of infallibility to him in both legislative and advisory commands, and his protection from error in matters of both religion and the world.

The reason the objection does not apply is that the derivation of guidance from "choosing" implies his being guided to that for which the choosing occurred. Choosing only pertains to that which contains religious felicity, which is the restriction of servitude (العبودية) to Allah, the Glorified. Thus, the guidance also pertains to that; it is the guidance in which there is no intermediary between the Exalted and the guided servant, and it never fails, as He said: **فَإِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ يُضِلُّ** "for Allah does not guide those whom He leads astray" (16:37). As for guidance to the benefits of life, although it returns to the Exalted, it is among those things where causes intervene between it and Him, and causes sometimes fail. Understand this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ اهْبِطا مِنْها جَمِيعاً بَعْضُكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ** **"He said, ‘Get down both of you from it, all together, being enemies of one another’" (20:123).** The commentary on the like of this has already been provided in Surahs *al-Baqarah* and *al-A'raf*.

In His saying: **قالَ اهْبِطا** "He said, ‘Get down both of you’" there is a transition (التفات) from speaking in the first person plural to the third person singular. Perhaps the reason for this is that the verse contains a decree (القضاء) and a judgment (الحكم), which are exclusive to the Exalted. He said: **وَاللهُ يَقْضِي بِالْحَقِّ** "And Allah judges with the truth" (40:20), and He said: **إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ** "Judgment belongs only to Allah" (12:67).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ مِنِّي هُدىً فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدايَ فَلا يَضِلُّ وَلا يَشْقى** **"then if there comes to you any guidance from Me, those who follow My guidance shall neither go astray nor be miserable" (20:123).** In the verse is a decree from the Exalted derived from the descent; therefore, it was coordinated with the "fa" of derivation (فاء التفريع). The origin of His saying: **فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ** "then if there comes to you" is "if there comes to you" (إن يأتكم) with the addition of "ma" (ما) and the "nun" of emphasis to indicate the occurrence of the condition. It is as if it were said: "If there comes to you guidance from Me—and it is inevitably coming—then whoever follows," etc.

In His saying: **فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدايَ** "those who follow My guidance," the attribution of following to the guidance is by way of a metonymic metaphor (استعارة بالكناية). Its origin is: "whoever follows the Guide who guides by My guidance."

His saying: **فَلا يَضِلُّ وَلا يَشْقى** "shall neither go astray nor be miserable" means he shall not stray in his path nor be wretched in his goal, which is the end of his affair. The absolute nature of straying and wretchedness necessitates the negation of both in the world and the Hereafter together. This is indeed the case, for divine guidance is the primordial religion (الدين الفطري) to which He called through the tongues of His Prophets. The religion of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — is the sum of beliefs and actions to which the human nature and creation call, according to the faculties with which he has been equipped. It is well-known that the felicity of everything is what its creation demands through its equipment; there is no felicity for it beyond that. The Exalted said: **فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها لا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللهِ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ** "So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the nature of Allah on which He originated mankind. There is no altering Allah’s creation. That is the upright religion" (30:30).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَنْ ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنْكاً وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ أَعْمى** **"But whoever turns away from My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life, and We shall raise him blind on the Day of Resurrection" (20:124).** Al-Raghib said: "Life (العيش) is the life specific to animals; it is more specific than 'life' (الحياة) because 'life' is said of animals, the Creator, and the angels. From it is derived 'livelihood' (المعيشة) for that by which one lives. The Exalted said: **نَحْنُ قَسَمْنا بَيْنَهُمْ مَعِيشَتَهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا** 'It is We who have dispensed their livelihood among them in the life of this world' (43:32), and **مَعِيشَةً ضَنْكاً** 'a wretched life.'" End quote. Narrowness (الضنك) is the tightness of anything; it is the same for masculine and feminine. It is said: "a narrow place" and "a narrow life." It is originally the verbal noun of **ضنك يضنك** (it became narrow).

His saying: **وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَنْ ذِكْرِي** "But whoever turns away from My remembrance" stands opposite to His saying in the previous verse: **فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدايَ** "those who follow My guidance." The requirement of the opposition was to say: "and whoever does not follow My guidance," but it was diverted to mentioning the turning away from the remembrance to point thereby to the cause of the judgment. For forgetting the Exalted and turning away from His remembrance is the cause for the narrowness of life and blindness on the Day of Resurrection, and to serve as a preparation for what will be mentioned regarding the Exalted forgetting on the Day of Resurrection the one who forgot Him in the world.

The meaning of His remembrance (ذكره) is either the verbal noun—thus His saying: **ذِكْرِي** "My remembrance" is the addition of the verbal noun to its object—or the Quran, or all the heavenly books, as supported by His subsequent saying: **أَتَتْكَ آياتُنا فَنَسِيتَها** "Our signs came to you and you forgot them" (20:126). Or it is the true call, and its naming as "remembrance" is because the necessary consequence of following it and taking hold of it is the remembrance of the Exalted.

His saying: **فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنْكاً** "his shall be a wretched life" means a narrow one. This is because whoever forgets his Lord and is cut off from His remembrance has nothing left but to cling to the world and make it his sole objective for which he strives, concerning himself with improving his livelihood, expanding it, and enjoying it. The livelihood he is given does not suffice him, whether it be little or much; for whenever he obtains and acquires some of it, his soul is not satisfied with it, and it is pulled toward acquiring what is more and wider without stopping at any limit. Thus, he is always in a state of constriction of the chest and rage at what he has found, his heart attached to what is beyond it, along with the onslaught of anxiety, grief, sorrow, restlessness, agitation, and fear of the descent of calamities and the occurrence of accidents such as death, illness, disability, the envy of the envious, the plot of the plotter, the failure of effort, and the parting of a loved one.

Had he known the station of his Lord, remembering and not forgetting, he would have been certain that he has a life with his Lord that is not mixed with death, a kingdom that is not subject to cessation, a might that is not tainted by humiliation, and a joy, happiness, elevation, and dignity that cannot be measured by any measure nor end at any term; and that the world is a house of passage and its life in the Hereafter is but a brief enjoyment. If he knew that, his soul would be content with what was decreed for him of the world, and the livelihood he was given would suffice him without narrowness or wretchedness.

It has been said: The meaning of the "wretched life" is the punishment of the grave and the misery of the intermediate life (الحياة البرزخية), based on the fact that many of those who turn away from the remembrance of Allah may attain the widest of livelihoods and the affairs of the world may be handed to them by their reins, so they are in a wide, happy life.

However, this is based on comparing the livelihood of the rich with the livelihood of the poor by looking at the livelihoods themselves and the possibilities within them. The Quranic view does not pertain to them from this angle at all. Rather, the verses discuss them by comparing the livelihood attributed to the believer—who is armed with the remembrance of Allah and faith in Him—with the livelihood attributed to the disbeliever who forgets his Lord, whose soul is attached to the worldly life, and who is stripped of faith. There is no doubt that the believer has a free, happy life; what his Lord has honored him with of livelihood suffices him, even if it be with mere sufficiency or less. As for the one who turns away from the remembrance of his Lord, he has nothing but dissatisfaction with what he has found and attachment to what is beyond it.

Indeed, the punishment of the grave is among the instances of the "wretched life," based on His saying: **فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنْكاً** "his shall be a wretched life" being directed toward explaining their state in the world, and His saying: **وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ أَعْمى** "and We shall raise him blind on the Day of Resurrection" toward explaining their state in the Hereafter, with the intermediate realm (البرزخ) being an extension of the world.

It has also been said: The "wretched life" refers to the punishment of the Fire on the Day of Resurrection, and His saying: **وَنَحْشُرُهُ** "and We shall raise him," etc., refers to what precedes the entry into the Fire.

However, the absolute nature of His saying: **فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنْكاً** "his shall be a wretched life" and then the restriction of His saying: **وَنَحْشُرُهُ** "and We shall raise him" to the Day of Resurrection does not harmonize with this, as is evident.

Indeed, if the beginning of the verse is taken as absolute, including the livelihood of both the world and the Hereafter, and its end—due to its restriction to the Day of Resurrection—is specific to the Hereafter, it would have a basis.

His saying: **وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ أَعْمى** "and We shall raise him blind on the Day of Resurrection" means such that he is not guided to that which contains his felicity, which is the Garden. The evidence for this is what follows in the two subsequent verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ رَبِّ لِمَ حَشَرْتَنِي أَعْمى وَقَدْ كُنْتُ بَصِيراً** **"He will say, ‘My Lord! Why have You raised me blind, when I used to be seeing?’" (20:125).** It occurs to the mind that the blindness of the Day of Resurrection pertains to the sight of the senses, for what he asks about is the loss of the sight he had in the world, which is the sight of the senses, rather than the sight of the heart, which is insight (البصيرة). This poses a difficulty with the apparent meaning of what indicates that the criminals will see on the Day of Resurrection the terrors of the day and the signs of majesty and subjugation, such as His saying: **إِذِ الْمُجْرِمُونَ ناكِسُوا رُؤُسِهِمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ رَبَّنا أَبْصَرْنا وَسَمِعْنا** "when the guilty hang their heads before their Lord [saying], ‘Our Lord! We have seen and heard’" (32:12), and His saying: **اقْرَأْ كِتابَكَ** "Read your book" (17:14). For this reason, some have mentioned that they are raised seeing and then become blind, and others that they are raised seeing, then blind, then seeing.

This is an analogy of the matters and states of the Hereafter with their counterparts in the world, and it is an analogy with a difference. For it is evident and accepted from the Book and the Sunnah that the system governing the Hereafter is different from the system governing the world that we are familiar with from nature. The fact that the seeing person sees everything visible and the blind person does not perceive everything that is meant to be seen—as witnessed in the worldly system—has no proof for its universality in the otherworldly system. It is possible for the matter to be partial there; thus, the criminal would be blind, not seeing what contains the felicity of his life, his success, and his attainment of dignity, while he witnesses that by which the proof is established against him, what terrifies him of the horrors of the Resurrection, and what intensifies the punishment upon him of the Fire and other things. The Exalted said: **إِنَّهُمْ عَنْ رَبِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَمَحْجُوبُونَ** "No indeed! They will be barred from their Lord on that day" (83:15).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ كَذلِكَ أَتَتْكَ آياتُنا فَنَسِيتَها وَكَذلِكَ الْيَوْمَ تُنْسى** **"He will say, ‘So it is. Our signs came to you and you forgot them, and thus you will be forgotten today’" (20:126).** The verse is the answer to the questioner’s question: **رَبِّ لِمَ حَشَرْتَنِي أَعْمى وَقَدْ كُنْتُ بَصِيراً** "My Lord! Why have You raised me blind, when I used to be seeing?" The indication in His saying: **كَذلِكَ أَتَتْكَ** "So it is. [Our signs] came to you" is to his being raised blind as mentioned in the question. In His saying: **وَكَذلِكَ الْيَوْمَ** "and thus [you will be forgotten] today," it refers to the meaning of His saying: **أَتَتْكَ آياتُنا فَنَسِيتَها** "Our signs came to you and you forgot them." The meaning is: He said: "Just as We raised you blind, Our signs came to you and you forgot them; and just as Our signs came to you and you forgot them, We forget you today." That is: your being raised today blind and your being left not seeing anything is like your leaving Our signs in the world as a forgotten thing is left; and your lack of being guided by them is like Our leaving you today and not guiding you by making you seeing so that you might be guided to salvation. In other words: We have only recompensed you on this day with the like of what you did in the world, as the Exalted said: **وَجَزاءُ سَيِّئَةٍ سَيِّئَةٌ مِثْلُها** "The recompense of evil is an evil like it" (42:40).

Allah, the Glorified, has named the disobedience of the criminals—who are those who turn away from His remembrance and abandon His guidance—as "forgetting" His signs, and their recompense by blinding them on the Day of Resurrection as "forgetting" from Him toward them. With this, the end of the discourse turns back to its beginning, which is the disobedience of Adam that He named "forgetting" His covenant when He said: **وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنا إِلى آدَمَ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَنَسِيَ** "Certainly We had enjoined Adam before; but he forgot." It is as if the story of Adam’s Garden, with its specificities, was a prior example representing what would occur to his children after him until the Day of Resurrection. Thus, His prohibition against approaching the tree represents the religious call and divine guidance afterward, and his disobedience—which was a forgetting of the covenant—represents the disobediences of his children, which are a forgetting of the remembrance of the Exalted and His reminding signs. The only difference is that Adam’s trial was before the legislation of the laws; thus, the prohibition directed toward him was advisory (إرشادي), and the contravention he was tried with was of the nature of "abandoning the better" (ترك الأولى), unlike the case with his children.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنا إِلى آدَمَ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَنَسِيَ وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُ عَزْماً** — He said: "Regarding what He forbade him from, which was eating from the tree."

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, narrated from Jamil ibn Darraj from some of our companions from one of the two [Imams] (peace be upon them both), he said: I asked him: "How did Allah take Adam to task for forgetfulness?" He replied: "He did not forget. How could he forget when he was remembering it, and Iblis was saying to him: **ما نَهاكُما رَبُّكُما عَنْ هذِهِ الشَّجَرَةِ إِلَّا أَنْ تَكُونا مَلَكَيْنِ أَوْ تَكُونا مِنَ الْخالِدِينَ** 'Your Lord has only forbidden you from this tree lest you should become angels, or lest you should become immortal'?"

**I say:** This is the saying of those who say regarding the verse that forgetfulness is in its literal sense, and that Adam truly forgot the prohibition at the time of eating and had no intention of disobedience at all. He (peace be upon him) refuted that by its contradiction with the Book. By this, the weakness of what was narrated in *Rawdat al-Kafi* becomes apparent, with its chain to Abu Hamzah from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "Indeed Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, enjoined Adam not to approach this tree. When the time reached that which was in the knowledge of Allah that he would eat from it, he forgot and ate from it. This is the saying of Allah, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنا إِلى آدَمَ مِنْ قَبْلُ فَنَسِيَ وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُ عَزْماً**."

This view is attributed to Ibn Abbas, and its origin is what was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from al-Zubayr ibn Bakkar in *al-Muwaffaqiyyat* from Ibn Abbas, who said: I asked Umar ibn al-Khattab about the saying of Allah: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَسْئَلُوا عَنْ أَشْياءَ إِنْ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ** "O you who have faith! Do not ask about things which, if they are disclosed to you, will upset you" (5:101). He said: "There were men from the Emigrants (المهاجرين) who had something [questionable] in their lineages, so they said one day: 'By Allah, we wish that Allah would reveal a Quran regarding our lineage.' So Allah revealed what you read."

Then he said to me: "Indeed this companion of yours—meaning Ali ibn Abi Talib—if he were to lead, he would be ascetic; but I fear the vanity of his soul might carry him away." I said: "O Commander of the Faithful, our companion is who you know. By Allah, we do not say that he changed or deviated, nor did he anger the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) during the days of his companionship." He said: "Not even regarding the daughter of Abu Jahl, when he wanted to propose to her over Fatimah?" I said: "Allah said regarding the disobedience of Adam (peace be upon him): **وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُ عَزْماً** 'and We did not find any resoluteness in him'—and our companion did not resolve to anger the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), but these are passing thoughts (الخواطر) that no one is able to repel from himself, and they may occur to the jurist in the religion of Allah, the one knowledgeable of the command of Allah. When he is alerted to them, he returns and repents." He said: "O Ibn Abbas, whoever thinks he can repel your seas—and dive into them with you until he reaches their bottom—has thought of an impossibility."

He built his argument on the fact that the intended meaning of "resoluteness" (العزم) is the resolution to disobey, and its necessary consequence is that "forgetfulness" (النسيان) is in its literal sense. Thus, Adam did not remember the covenant while eating, nor did he resolve to disobey; so he did not disobey his Lord. It has already been mentioned that this contradicts His saying: **قالَ ما نَهاكُما رَبُّكُما عَنْ هذِهِ الشَّجَرَةِ إِلَّا أَنْ تَكُونا مَلَكَيْنِ أَوْ تَكُونا مِنَ الْخالِدِينَ**. Furthermore, the verse with the meaning he mentioned does not suit the context of the verses preceding or following it. It is proper to exonerate Ibn Abbas—being who he is—from having this view attributed to him.

As for what occurred in the hadith regarding the anger of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) toward Ali (peace be upon him) in his desire to propose to the daughter of Abu Jahl over Fatimah (peace be upon her), it is an allusion to what is in *Sahih al-Bukhari* and *Sahih Muslim* through several paths from al-Miswar ibn Makhramah. The wording of some is: "That Ali ibn Abi Talib proposed to the daughter of Abu Jahl—while he had Fatimah, the daughter of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—and when Fatimah heard of that, she came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said to him: 'Your people are talking that you do not get angry for your daughters—and here is Ali marrying the daughter of Abu Jahl.'" Al-Miswar said: "So the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) stood up, and I heard him when he recited the testimony (تشهد)—then he said: 'As for what follows, I married [my daughter] to Abu al-As ibn al-Rabi'—and he spoke to me and was truthful to me (1), and Fatimah is a part of me—and I only dislike that they should try her—and by Allah, the daughter of the Messenger of Allah and the daughter of the enemy of Allah shall never be gathered under one man.'" He said: "So Ali abandoned the proposal."

Deep reflection upon what the hadith contains necessitates a bad suspicion of it, for it contains an explicit defamation of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). If what it contains were true, the anger from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) would be a pre-Islamic impulse (نزعة جاهلية) without a justifying cause. For what would he be angry at him? Was it for His saying, the Exalted: **فَانْكِحُوا ما طابَ لَكُمْ مِنَ النِّساءِ مَثْنى وَثُلاثَ وَرُباعَ** "then marry of women as may be agreeable to you, two, or three, or four" (4:3), which is a general verse that was not abrogated nor specified by another verse specific to her? Or was it by something from the Sunnah that specifies the verse for Fatimah (peace be upon her) and legislates for her specifically a personal ruling of prohibition, which was not established nor conveyed before that? The wording (2) of the hadith contains an indication of that. Or was this very saying an explanation and conveyance, having not been explained or conveyed before that? There is no harm in contravening a ruling before its conveyance, and there is no disobedience in it. So what is the meaning of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) anger toward one who did not commit a disobedience nor resolve upon it? His (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) station is far removed from these pre-Islamic traits. It is as if some narrators of the hadith intended to defame Ali (peace be upon him), and so they defamed the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) without realizing it.

Furthermore, it contradicts the definitive narrations indicating the purity of Ali’s (peace be upon him) station from disobedience, such as the Hadith of the Two Weighty Things (الثقلين), the Hadith of the Position (المنزلة), and the Hadith "Ali is with the truth and the truth is with Ali," among others.

In *al-Kafi* and *al-Ilal*, narrated with a chain from Jabir ibn Yazid from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): Regarding the saying of Allah, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنا إِلى آدَمَ مِنْ قَبْلُ ـ فَنَسِيَ وَلَمْ نَجِدْ لَهُ عَزْماً** — He said: "He enjoined him regarding Muhammad and the Imams from his progeny, but he left it—and he did not have resoluteness in them that it is thus. They were only named 'the Possessors of Resoluteness' (أولو العزم) because they were enjoined regarding Muhammad and the successors after him and the Mahdi and his conduct—so their resoluteness gathered that it is thus and [they gathered] the acknowledgment of it."

**I say:** The narration is a summary of a detailed hadith narrated in *al-Kafi* from Muhammad ibn Yahya from Ahmad ibn Muhammad from Dawud al-Ajli from Zurarah from Humran from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), in which he mentions the beginning of the creation of the human, then the witnessing of the people over themselves in the world of particles (عالم الذر), and the taking of the covenant from Adam (peace be upon him) and from the Possessors of Resoluteness among the Messengers regarding Lordship (الربوبية), Prophethood (النبوة), and Guardianship (الولاية); the acknowledgment of the Possessors of Resoluteness of that, and the hesitation of Adam (peace be upon him) and his lack of resoluteness in acknowledgment, even if he did not deny; then the application of His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنا إِلى آدَمَ** the verse, upon it.

The meaning mentioned in the narration is from the inner depth (بطن) of the Quran, in which rulings are returned to their reality and covenants to their interpretation (تأويل), which is the Divine Guardianship (الولاية الإلهية). It is not part of the linguistic commentary (تفسير) of the verse at all. The proof that it is not commentary is that the verses—which are twelve verses—recount a single story. If the first verse were carried upon this meaning as commentary, there would remain nothing in the verses to indicate the prohibition against eating from the tree, which is the pillar of the story upon which the rest depends. His saying: **فَلا يُخْرِجَنَّكُما مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ** "So do not let him expel you from paradise" does not suffice for it, as is evident. The mentioned prohibition was not mentioned in a Surah preceding this one in revelation such that it could be referred to; and Surahs *al-A'raf* and *al-Baqarah*, in which the mentioned prohibition is stated, are later in revelation than this Surah, as will be pointed out, Allah willing.

In short, it is from the inner depth rather than the commentary, even if it occurred in some narrations in the form of commentary, like the narration of Jabir previously. Perhaps it is among what was confused by some narrators of the hadith, so they reported it in this form. The matter reached the point in some narrations that the meaning mentioned by the Imam was made a part of the verse, thus becoming among the reports of distortion (التحريف), as in *al-Manaqib* from al-Baqir (peace be upon him): "**وَلَقَدْ عَهِدْنا إِلى آدَمَ مِنْ قَبْلُ** words regarding Muhammad, Ali, Fatimah, al-Hasan, al-Husayn—and the Imams from their progeny; thus it was revealed to Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)."

Similar to these narrations are other narrations in which the application of His saying, the Exalted: **فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدايَ** and His saying: **عَنْ ذِكْرِي** occurred upon the guardianship (ولاية) of the People of the House (عليه السلام). These are from the narrations of application (الجري) — *the principle of applying a verse to new instances over time* — rather than commentary, as has been imagined.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Abi Shaybah, al-Tabarani, Abu Nu'aym in *al-Hilyah*, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Whoever follows the Book of Allah—Allah guides him from straying in the world and protects him from the evil of the reckoning on the Day of Resurrection. That is because Allah says: **فَمَنِ اتَّبَعَ هُدايَ ـ فَلا يَضِلُّ وَلا يَشْقى**."

**I say:** The hadith applies His saying, the Exalted: **فَلا يَضِلُّ** "shall [not] go astray" to the world, and His saying: **وَلا يَشْقى** "nor be miserable" to the Hereafter; thus it supports what was previously stated in the commentary of the verse.

In *al-Majma'*: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنْكاً** — "And it was said: It is the punishment of the grave; narrated from Ibn Mas'ud, Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, and al-Suddi, and Abu Hurayrah narrated it as a raised (مرفوعاً) report."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Abu Basir, who said: I heard Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) saying: "Whoever dies while he is healthy and wealthy—and did not perform the Hajj—he is among those of whom Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: **وَنَحْشُرُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ أَعْمى**." He said: I said: "Glory be to Allah! Blind?" He said: "Yes, Allah blinds him from the path of truth."

**I say:** Al-Qummi narrated the like of it in his commentary with a chain from Mu'awiyah ibn Ammar, and al-Saduq in *Man La Yahduruhu al-Faqih* as a disconnected (مرسلاً) report from him from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him). The narration, in its specification of the blindness of the Day of Resurrection to the path of truth—which is the path of salvation and felicity—supports what was previously stated in the commentary of the verse.

---
(1) Praise for his son-in-law Abu al-As, the husband of his daughter Zaynab.
(2) And in what al-Miswar narrated through another path: "I do not prohibit a lawful thing nor make lawful a prohibited thing, but by Allah, the daughter of the Messenger of Allah and the daughter of the enemy of Allah shall never be gathered in one place ever."
